skill.name: wrangler
skill.description: Cloudflare Wrangler reference for deploying, developing, and managing Workers and related Cloudflare resources.
skill.summary: Guides Wrangler CLI use for Cloudflare Workers development, deployment, bindings, secrets, and resource management.
skill.author: cloudflare

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows the file is Markdown documentation for Wrangler CLI usage. No prompt injection, hidden executable payload, or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The skill still has medium operational risk because users may run commands that deploy, delete, list, or modify Cloudflare resources and secrets.

medium_findings.title: Operational Cloudflare Resource Changes
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Wrangler commands that can deploy Workers, delete namespaces, delete buckets, modify data, trigger workflows, and terminate workflow instances. This is legitimate for a Wrangler guide, but execution against authenticated accounts can affect production resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced sections contain direct Wrangler examples for deploy, delete, put, trigger, and terminate actions. The intent is documented administration, not hidden malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Secret Management Commands Require Caution
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for local secret files, Wrangler secret commands, public registry credentials, and Secrets Store operations. These examples are normal for Cloudflare workflows, but users must avoid exposing real credentials in prompts, logs, or committed files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly discuss API keys, database URLs, secret commands, and AWS credential placeholders. The content uses placeholders and warnings, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Remote Development Can Touch Real Services
medium_findings.description: The guide explains remote bindings and remote development modes that connect local work to real Cloudflare resources. This is expected Wrangler behavior, but it can cause live service access and usage charges.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly states that remote mode runs on the Cloudflare edge and that selected bindings connect to real resources. No evidence shows unauthorized network destinations.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code fences and inline Wrangler commands. They are not embedded scripts, Ruby backticks, or automatically executed code in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines are fenced Markdown examples and a command table. There is no executable wrapper or dynamic command construction in the file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts correspond to the word describe in workflow commands, not MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines contain Wrangler describe commands. No cryptographic algorithm or implementation appears at those locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links or Localhost Tests
low_findings.description: The URL findings are a Cloudflare documentation link and localhost scheduled-event examples. They do not send secrets to third-party endpoints or create a data exfiltration path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected URLs are Cloudflare documentation and localhost endpoints. No credential-bearing request to an external host was found.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Alert Is Local Storage Troubleshooting Text
low_findings.description: The browser storage finding refers to Wrangler local storage persistence troubleshooting. It does not instruct access to browser storage APIs or extraction of browser data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is a troubleshooting table entry about a local Wrangler state directory. No browser storage API or credential collection behavior is present.

content.value_statement: Cloudflare Workers projects often require exact Wrangler syntax across many services. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused command reference for safer setup, deployment, and resource management.
actual_capabilities: Provides Wrangler commands for local development, deployment, validation, and live logging.
Shows wrangler.jsonc patterns for Workers, environments, bindings, cron triggers, and observability.
Documents setup and management commands for KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Queues, and Workflows.
Explains Wrangler secrets, Secrets Store usage, and local development secret files.
Covers Pages deployment, container registry commands, Workers AI, and testing with Vitest.
limitations: It is a command reference, not a replacement for Cloudflare account permissions or Wrangler authentication.
It does not verify whether commands match the latest installed Wrangler version.
It cannot prevent accidental changes to production resources if users run destructive commands.
It does not include complete application code for every Cloudflare service.

use_cases.title: Prepare Worker Deployments
use_cases.description: Create or review Wrangler commands for validating, dry-running, and deploying Workers across staging and production environments.

use_cases.title: Configure Cloudflare Bindings
use_cases.description: Build wrangler.jsonc guidance for KV, R2, D1, Queues, Hyperdrive, Vectorize, Workers AI, and environment-specific bindings.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Local Development
use_cases.description: Find commands for Wrangler dev mode, type generation, config validation, scheduled-event testing, and common setup errors.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Wrangler Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Cloudflare Worker setup and list the Wrangler commands I should run before deploying.

prompt_templates.title: Create Binding Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add Wrangler configuration for KV, R2, D1, and a staging environment. Explain each binding briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe Wrangler deployment sequence with validation, type generation, dry run, staging deploy, and production deploy.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Cloudflare Resource Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these planned Wrangler commands for destructive actions, secret exposure, remote resource access, and safer alternatives.

output_examples.input: I need to deploy a Worker safely to staging first.
output: A staged deployment checklist with authentication, config validation, type generation, dry-run validation, staging deployment, and log review.
Clear warnings for commands that affect production resources.

output_examples.input: I need D1 and R2 bindings in my Worker configuration.
output: A concise configuration plan naming the D1 database binding, R2 bucket binding, environment split, and follow-up type generation step.
Notes about local versus remote binding behavior.

output_examples.input: I want to manage Worker secrets without leaking values.
output: Recommended secret handling steps using interactive commands and local development secret files.
Warnings to avoid committed secret files, prompt exposure, and shell history leaks.

best_practices: Run validation, type generation, and dry-run deployment before changing production resources.
Use separate Wrangler environments for staging and production.
Keep real secrets out of prompts, logs, source control, and shared transcripts.
anti_patterns: Do not run delete, rollback, or bulk update commands without confirming the target account and environment.
Do not paste real API keys, database URLs, or registry credentials into AI prompts.
Do not assume local development uses remote resources unless the binding configuration says so.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code provide accurate Wrangler commands and configuration guidance for Cloudflare Workers projects.

faq.question: Can it deploy resources by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance. Actual deployment requires a user environment with Wrangler installed and authenticated.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use with production accounts?
faq.answer: Use caution. It includes commands that can deploy, delete, roll back, or modify live Cloudflare resources.

faq.question: Does it cover Cloudflare service bindings?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers common bindings for KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Queues, Workers AI, Workflows, and Secrets Store.

faq.question: Should I share real secrets in prompts?
faq.answer: No. Use placeholders in prompts and set real values through Wrangler or trusted local files.

faq.question: Does it replace Cloudflare documentation?
faq.answer: No. It is a focused reference. Check official Cloudflare documentation for version-specific changes and service limits.
